cont_clean: A recent USDA Office of Inspector General review found $13.3 million in potential SNAP payment issues in Ohio after analyzing just one year of participant data. When you scale that finding to California, where CalFresh serves over 5.3 million people and distributes about $12.2 billion in benefits annually, the magnitude grows rapidly. Applying a similar pattern of questionable payments suggests California could see tens of millions of dollars per year, roughly $40–$50 million, in improper SNAP payments if subjected to the same level of data scrutiny. And when viewed against broader federal estimates showing double-digit improper payment rates across the program nationwide, the potential exposure in a state as large as California becomes even more significant. That is why organizations like the California Welfare Fraud Investigators Association (CWFIA) have long emphasized the need for stronger data matching, cross-state coordination, and modernized verification tools to identify and prevent improper payments before they go out the door. As SNAP continues to operate at historic scale, proactive oversight and enforcement are essential not just to protect taxpayer dollars, but to ensure resources are directed correctly. Expanding the kinds of reviews conducted in Ohio to large states like California could uncover substantially larger volumes of improper payments and reinforce the importance of continued policy and technology improvements. cont_clean: The Republican Megabill's SNAP cuts are hitting under-resourced families hard — and we're seeing it firsthand in the Washington, DC Metro region. Since the bill was signed into law on July 4, 2025, SNAP participation has fallen sharply across the country. The @centeronbudget has built a powerful state-by-state tracker showing exactly who is losing coverage and where (link in bio). In the DMV, the data tells a stark story. SNAP participation has fallen 13% in Virginia. In DC, we anticipate a 10–15% decrease in the coming months as additional cuts and work requirements take effect this summer. All of our farmers markets accept SNAP, and our dollar-for-dollar incentive match, FreshMatch, offers up to $30 per day. Despite that, SNAP spending at our markets is down 21% from January through April 2026 compared to the same period last year. That drop has real consequences: ➡️ Shoppers who lost SNAP benefits face reduced access to fresh, local food — often replaced by nothing at all. ➡️ Shoppers with reduced benefits face harder choices, often steering toward cheaper, less nutritious options. ➡️ Farmers and food producers lose revenue from both SNAP and FreshMatch sales, weakening regional food security. FRESHFARM is fortunate to operate other food access programs — including Produce Plus, in partnership with @DCHealth — that provide additional pathways to fresh food. But no local program can fully absorb the impact of federal policy changes of this magnitude. The harm to individuals, families, farmers, and the local food economy is real and growing. 📣 For those working in food systems and food access: what are you seeing in your communities? Policy Matters Ohio Researcher Molly Bryden said the "no tax on tips" provision gives the biggest tax break to those with the highest tipped wages. "Because about a third of tipped workers make too little to actually owe federal income taxes in the first place," Bryden said in an interview. cont_clean: The tax code recognizes children through the Child Tax Credit.​ ​ Good.​ ​ But child-care-specific benefits work differently. They mostly help when parents pay someone else for care so they can work or look for work.​ ​ Why can't a spouse who stays home receive the same kind of tax benefit for caring for his or her own children?​ ​ Because the system is not just asking, "Are children being cared for?"​ ​ It is asking, "Is this care arrangement keeping adults attached to taxable wages?"​ ​ That is the real issue.​ ​ A family using daycare so both parents can work fits neatly into the model.​ ​ A family using one income, part-time work, grandparent care, or integrated work from home does not.​ ​ So when people say women have "choices," ask the next question:​ ​ Are those choices actually supported?​ ​ Or does the system quietly favor the arrangements that keep more adults in the paid labor force?​ ​ A choice you cannot afford is mostly theater.​ Words: @jilliantymo #FamilyPolicy #ChildCare #ChildTaxCredit #WorkLifeBalance #ParentingChoices #TaxBenefits #WomenInWorkforce #FamilySupport #Motherhood
